learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Two Pointers, Greedy Algorithms, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine a rescue operation during a flood. Every rescue boat can carry at most two people and has a maximum weight limit. To minimize the number of rescue trips, the rescue team always tries to pair the lightest remaining person with the heaviest remaining person whenever possible.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine a rescue operation during a flood. Every rescue boat can carry at most two people and has a maximum weight limit. To minimize the number of rescue trips, the rescue team always tries to pair the lightest remaining person with the heaviest remaining person whenever possible.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Greedy Two Pointer pattern whenever you need to minimize the number of pairings under a weight or capacity constraint after sorting the data.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks to minimize the number of pairings under a capacity constraint, think of sorting followed by the Greedy Two Pointer technique.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an array people where people[i] is the weight of the ith person, and an integer limit representing the maximum weight a boat can carry. Each boat can carry at most two people at the same time, provided the sum of their weights does not exceed the limit. Return the minimum number of boats required to rescue everyone.

examples.explanation: No valid pairing except each heavy person alone.

edgeCases: All people have the same weight

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: For every unassigned person, search all remaining people to find the best partner. Repeat until everyone is assigned to a boat.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: The heaviest person must always board a boat. The only decision is whether the lightest remaining person can share that boat. If they fit together, send both. Otherwise, the heaviest person goes alone.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The heaviest remaining person must always be assigned a boat. Pair them with the lightest remaining person whenever possible.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks to minimize the number of pairings under a capacity constraint, think of sorting followed by the Greedy Two Pointer technique.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: The heaviest person must always be placed in a boat. Pairing them with the lightest available person whenever possible maximizes boat utilization and guarantees the minimum number of boats.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks to minimize the number of pairings under a capacity constraint, think of sorting followed by the Greedy Two Pointer technique.
commonWrongPaths: Trying to pair heavy people together.
Moving both pointers when the pair exceeds the limit.
Using left < right instead of left <= right.

hints: Try pairing the heaviest with the lightest.
commonMisconceptions: Trying to pair heavy people together.
Moving both pointers when the pair exceeds the limit.
Using left < right instead of left <= right.

pattern.name: Greedy + Two Pointers
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks to minimize the number of pairings under a capacity constraint, think of sorting followed by the Greedy Two Pointer technique.

approaches.title: Try Every Possible Pairing
approaches.idea: For every unassigned person, search all remaining people to find the best partner. Repeat until everyone is assigned to a boat.
algorithm: Search every remaining person for a valid partner.
approaches.dryRun:
people=[3,2,2,1]
Try every possible pairing.
Minimum boats = 3.

code.java: Brute force pairing is omitted because it becomes inefficient for large inputs.
code.python: Brute force solution omitted due to quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Sorting with Greedy Search
approaches.idea: Sort the array and greedily attempt to pair the heaviest person with the lightest available person.
algorithm: Attempt to pair lightest and heaviest.

approaches.title: Greedy Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Sort the weights. Use two pointers: one at the lightest person and one at the heaviest. If both fit within the boat limit, move both pointers. Otherwise, send the heaviest person alone. Every iteration uses exactly one boat.
approaches.intuition: The heaviest person must always be placed in a boat. Pairing them with the lightest available person whenever possible maximizes boat utilization and guarantees the minimum number of boats.
algorithm: If people[left] + people[right] <= limit, increment left.
approaches.dryRun:
people=[3,2,2,1], limit=3
Sorted=[1,2,2,3]
1+3>3 → Boat1=(3)
1+2=3 → Boat2=(1,2)
2 alone → Boat3=(2)
Answer=3

visualization.description: Always try to pair the lightest remaining person with the heaviest remaining person.

revisionNotes: Pair with the lightest if possible.

interviewFollowUps: What if each boat can carry three people?
Can this be solved without sorting?
Why is pairing the lightest with the heaviest optimal?
What if boats have different capacities?
Can this be extended to variable boat sizes?
interviewTips: Explain why the heaviest person must always be processed.
Justify pairing with the lightest person.
Mention that sorting enables the greedy decision.
State the O(n log n) complexity.
Discuss why the greedy approach is optimal.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The heaviest remaining person must always be assigned a boat. Pair them with the lightest remaining person whenever possible.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Sort → Two Pointers → Pair if possible → Otherwise send heaviest alone.

commonMistakes: Trying to pair heavy people together.
Moving both pointers when the pair exceeds the limit.
Using left < right instead of left <= right.

Nick White - Boats to Save People
